volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: The Little Carthaginian (Section 1.2.334 - Section 1.2.403)

sections.label: Section 1.2.334
sections.original: Eho , an irata est ? propitia hercle est . vel ego pro illa spondeo .
sections.translation: How now ? Is she angry , then ? Upon my faith , she is propitious . I will even answer for her .

sections.label: Section 1.2.335
sections.original: quid tu ais ?
sections.translation: What are you about ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.335b
sections.original: Quid mihi molestu'smolestus es , opsecro ?
sections.translation: Prithee , why are you annoying me ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.335c
sections.original: Aha , tam saeviter .
sections.translation: Thus cruel ? Alas !

sections.label: Section 1.2.336
sections.original: Mitte , amabo .
sections.translation: Let me go away , I beg of you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.336b
sections.original: Quid festinas ? turba nunc illi est .
sections.translation: Why in such a hurry ? There ’ s a crowd there at present .

sections.label: Section 1.2.336c
sections.original: Scio .
sections.translation: I know it ; there are other females there whom I wish to see , and by whom to be seen .

sections.label: Section 1.2.338
sections.original: Qui lubet spectare turpes , pulchram spectandam dare ?
sections.translation: How can it give you pleasure to look at ugly people , and to afford one so beauteous to be looked at ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.339
sections.original: Quia apud aedem Veneris hodie est mercatus meretricius :
sections.translation: Because to-day , at the Temple of Venus , there ’ s a fair for the courtesans ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.340
sections.original: eo conveniunt mercatores , ibi ego me ostendi volo .
sections.translation: there the dealers meet ; there I wish myself to be shown .

sections.label: Section 1.2.341
sections.original: Invendibili merci oportet ultro emptorem adducere :
sections.translation: To wares unsaleable , its right to entice the buyer of one ’ s own accord ; good wares easily meet with a purchaser , although they may be placed in concealment . How say you ? When , at my house here ( pointing ) will you lay your head and side by me ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.344
sections.original: Quo die Orcus Acherunte mortuos amiserit .
sections.translation: On the day on which Orcus sends away the dead [*] from Acheron .

sections.label: Section 1.2.345
sections.original: Sunt mihi intus nescio quot nummi aurei lymphatici .
sections.translation: I ’ ve got in-doors I know not how many golden coins in a state of madness .

sections.label: Section 1.2.346
sections.original: Deferto ad me , faxo actutum constiterit lymphaticum .
sections.translation: Bring them to me ; I ’ ll make their madness pretty soon come to an end .

sections.label: Section 1.2.347
sections.original: Bellula hercle .
sections.translation: A nice one , upon my word !

sections.label: Section 1.2.347b
sections.original: I díerecte in maxumam malam crucem .
sections.translation: Away to utter and extreme perdition with you , and go and be hanged !

sections.label: Section 1.2.348
sections.original: Quam magis aspecto , tam magis est nimbata et nugae merae .
sections.translation: The more I look at her , the more insignificant [*] she is , and a mere bauble .

sections.label: Section 1.2.349
sections.original: Segrega sermonem . taedet .
sections.translation: Keep your prating to yourself ; I ’ m tired of it .

sections.label: Section 1.2.349b
sections.original: Age , sustolle hoc amiculum .
sections.translation: ( To ADELPHASIUM .) Come , do lift up this outer garment .

sections.label: Section 1.2.350
sections.original: Pura sum , comperce amabo me attrectare , Agorastocles .
sections.translation: I ’ m in a state of purity [*] ; prithee forbear to touch me , Agorastocles .

sections.label: Section 1.2.351
sections.original: Quid agam nunc ?
sections.translation: What am I to do , then ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.351b
sections.original: Si sapias , curam hanc facere compendi potes .
sections.translation: If you are wise , you may be saving yourself your anxiety .

sections.label: Section 1.2.352
sections.original: Quid ? ego non te curem ? quid ais , Milphio ?
sections.translation: What ? Me not be anxious on your account ? What are you about , Milphio ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.352b
sections.original: Ecce odium meum .
sections.translation: See now , my aversion , this [*] . ( Aloud .) What is it you want with me ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.353b
sections.original: Cur mi haec irata est ?
sections.translation: Why is she angry with me ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.353c
sections.original: Cur haec irata est tibi ?
sections.translation: Why is she angry with you ? Why should I trouble myself about that ? For that is rather your own concern .

sections.label: Section 1.2.355
sections.original: Iam hercle tu periisti , nísi illam mihi tam tranquillam facis
sections.translation: On my word , it ’ s all over with you this very instant , if you don ’ t make her as smooth for me as the sea is at the time when the halcyon [*] is rearing her young ones there .

sections.label: Section 1.2.357
sections.original: Quid faciam ?
sections.translation: What am I to do ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.357b
sections.original: Exora , blandire , expalpa .
sections.translation: Entreat her , soothe , and flatter her .

sections.label: Section 1.2.357c
sections.original: Faciam sedulo .
sections.translation: I ’ ll do so with all diligence ; but see , please , that you don ’ t afterwards be giving this ambassador of yours a dressing with your fists .

sections.label: Section 1.2.359
sections.original: Non faciam .
sections.translation: I ’ ll not do so .

sections.label: Section 1.2.359b
sections.original: Non aequos in me es , sed morare et male facis .
sections.translation: Let ’ s now begone . ( AGORASTOCLES stands before her .) Do you detain me still ? You act badly ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.360
sections.original: bene promittis multa ex multis : omnia in cassum cadunt .
sections.translation: you make me many fair promises [*] ; of those many , the whole come to nothing at all . Not once , but a hundred times , have you sworn to give me my freedom . While depending on you , I have neither anywhere procured any other resources for myself , nor is this assistance of yours at all visible . And thus none the less am I still a slave . Move on , sister . ( To AGORASTOCLES .) Get you gone from me !

sections.label: Section 1.2.364b
sections.original: Perii . ecquid agis , Milphio ?
sections.translation: Utterly undone ! Come now , Milphio , what are you about ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.365
sections.original: Mea voluptas , mea delicia , mea vita , mea amoenitas ,
sections.translation: My joy , my delight , my life , my pleasure , apple of my eye , my little lip , my health , my sweet kiss , my honey , my heart , my biestings , my cream-cheese —

sections.label: Section 1.2.368
sections.original: Mene ego illaéc patiar praesente dici ? discrucior miser ,
sections.translation: Am I to allow these things to be said in my presence ? I ’ m quite distracted , wretch that I am , if I don ’ t order him at full speed to be hurried off to the executioner in a chariot and four !

sections.label: Section 1.2.370
sections.original: Noli , amabo , suscensere ero meo , causa mea .
sections.translation: Prithee , for my sake , don ’ t be angry with my master . I ’ ll make —

sections.label: Section 1.2.371
sections.original: ego faxo , si non irata es , ninnium pro te dabit
sections.translation: You are too cross . He ’ ll pay the money for you , and make you a citizen of Attica [*] , and a free woman .

sections.label: Section 1.2.373
sections.original: quin adire sínis ? quin tibi qui bene volunt , bene vis item ?
sections.translation: But why don ’ t you let me go away ? What is it you want ? Just as he wishes me well , in like manner do you wish me well .

sections.label: Section 1.2.374
sections.original: si ante quid mentitust , nunciam dehinc érit verax tibi .
sections.translation: If , indeed , he has deceived you before , from this time forward he shall be truthful to you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.375
sections.original: sine te exorem , sine prehendam auriculis , sine dem savium .
sections.translation: Do let me prevail upon you ; do let me take you by those little ears [*] ; do let me give you a kiss .

sections.label: Section 1.2.376
sections.original: Apscede hinc sis , sycophanta par ero .
sections.translation: Get you gone hence , will you , you trepanner .

sections.label: Section 1.2.376b
sections.original: At scin quo modo ?
sections.translation: I ’ ll obey you . But on what terms — do you understand ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.377
sections.original: iam hercle ego faciam ploratillum , nisi te facio propitiam ,
sections.translation: By my troth , I shall now set him a weeping , if I don ’ t make you kind ; and ( unless I do make you kind he certainly will do it ) I ’ m dreadfully afraid lest he should beat me . I know the harsh manners of this crabbed man .

sections.label: Section 1.2.380
sections.original: quam ob rem amabo , mea voluptas , sine te hoc exorarier .
sections.translation: Wherefore , my delight , pray do let me prevail upon you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.381
sections.original: Non ego homo trioboli sum , nisi ego illi mastigiae
sections.translation: I ’ m not a man worth threepence [*] , if I don ’ t tear out the eyes and teeth of that whip-scoundrel . ( He beats MILPHIO .) There ’ s your delight for you ! There ’ s your honey ! There ’ s your heart ! There are your biestings [*] ! There ’ s your health ! There ’ s your sweet kiss !

sections.label: Section 1.2.384
sections.original: Impias , ere , te : oratorem verberas .
sections.translation: Master , you are rendering yourself guilty of impiety ! You are beating an ambassador .

sections.label: Section 1.2.384b
sections.original: Iam istoc magis :
sections.translation: More than that even still .

sections.label: Section 1.2.385
sections.original: etiam ocellum addam et labellum et linguam .
sections.translation: ( Beating him again .) I shall now add the apple of the eye , the little lip too , and the tongue .

sections.label: Section 1.2.385b
sections.original: Ecquid facies modi ?
sections.translation: When will you be making an end ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.386
sections.original: sicine ego te orare iussi ?
sections.translation: Was it in that fashion I requested you to plead for me ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.386b
sections.original: Quo modo ergo orem ?
sections.translation: How then was I to plead ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.386c
sections.original: Rogas ?
sections.translation: Do you ask me that ? Why thus you should have said , you scoundrel : his delight , I do entreat of you , his honey , his heart , his little lip , his tongue , his sweet kiss , his biestings , his sweet cream-cheese , you whip-scoundrel .

sections.label: Section 1.2.391
sections.original: omnia illa , quae dicebas tua , ésse ea memorares mea .
sections.translation: All these things which you spoke of as yours , you should have mentioned as mine .

sections.label: Section 1.2.392
sections.original: Opsecro hercle te , voluptas huius atque odium meum ,
sections.translation: By my troth , I do entreat you , his delight and my own aversion ; his full-bosomed mistress , my enemy and evil-wisher ; his eye , my eyesore ; his honey , my gall —

sections.label: Section 1.2.395
sections.original: ut tu huic irata ne sis aut , si id fieri non potest ,
sections.translation: don ’ t you be angry with him ; or , if that cannot be , do take a rope and hang yourself , with your master and your household : for I see that henceforth , on your account , I shall have to live upon sighing ; and as it is , I ’ ve already got my back about as hard with weals as an oyster-shell , by reason of your amours .

sections.label: Section 1.2.399b
sections.original: Amabo , men prohibere postulas
sections.translation: Prithee , do you wish me to hinder him

sections.label: Section 1.2.400
sections.original: ne te verberet magis quám ne mendax me advorsum siet ?
sections.translation: from beating you , rather than that he should not prove untrue towards me ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.401
sections.original: Aliquid huic responde , amabo , commode , ne incommodus
sections.translation: Do answer him in somewhat kindly terms , there ’ s a dear , that he mayn ’ t be annoying to us ; for he ’ s detaining us from our purpose .

sections.label: Section 1.2.403
sections.original: Verum . etiam tibi hanc amittam noxiam unam , Agorastocles .
sections.translation: That ’ s true . This one fault more will I pardon you for , Agorastocles . I am not angry .
